identity.title: Farm Labor Contractors and Crew Leaders

lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. operating companies primarily supplying crews or individual workers for agricultural production or manual harvesting, including recruiting, hiring, dispatch, supervision, payroll, worker transport or housing when authorized, within the specified EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Growers directly employing their own labor, agricultural associations acting only for members, temporary staffing outside agriculture, farm management, machine harvesting, postharvest packing, labor-only sole proprietors without a transferable company, captive affiliates, non-control financing vehicles, and operations without transferable control.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring fees from farms, orchards, vineyards, nurseries, and agricultural associations for furnished workers or crews, commonly quoted per worker-hour, crew-hour, day, acre, unit harvested, or managed season; customer payments fund worker wages, payroll burden, transport, housing or other pass-through costs, compliance, supervision, and contractor margin.
lens.deviation_from_default: The frozen labor-intensity ratio of 2.2759 is economically anomalous: QCEW wages plus the fixed benefit multiplier exceed SUSB receipts by more than two times. Sector-specific coverage, agricultural-production-worker scope, pass-through accounting, establishment classification, and data-vintage differences make that input unsuitable as a literal compensation share, so the automation parameter is anchored to occupational mix and task evidence instead.

a.bridge: BLS reports that 67.2% of employment in the broader support-activities-for-crop-production industry is in farming occupations and 56.1% is crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers, while management is only 2.67%. DOL rules identify digitally assistable registration, disclosure, payroll-record, recruiting, authorization, transport, and housing workflows. I translate the broader occupational mix and regulated workflows into wage-weighted task opportunity for labor contractors, discounting heavily because the furnished workers' planting, pruning, harvesting, packing, loading, and field supervision are physical. The frozen labor-intensity ratio is not used literally because its numerator exceeds receipts by 2.28 times and Census research documents coverage complications for agricultural production workers in support-service establishments.
a.rationale: AI can help recruit and match workers, forecast crew needs, schedule and dispatch, translate communications, extract documents, validate registrations, reconcile time and piece-rate records, prepare payroll and disclosures, route vehicles, and flag compliance exceptions. It cannot perform most field labor, drive or house workers without authorized accountable operators, resolve emergencies, or replace in-person crew leadership.
a.change_evidence: A 115115-specific task-and-compensation study separating furnished field-worker wages from contractor management, recruiting, payroll, transport, housing, and administration, with time studies and observed AI adoption, would materially change the range.
caveats: BLS occupational evidence covers NAICS 115100, not the six-digit contractor industry, and may include machine and technical services with different task mixes.
The frozen wage-to-receipts mismatch likely reflects coverage, accounting, or vintage differences, so it cannot be treated as a feasible labor share.

rho.rationale: Contractors already coordinate workers, farms, payroll, documents, vehicles, housing, and authorizations, making mobile and workflow software implementable. Multilingual and transient workforces, rural connectivity, piece-rate complexity, fragmented customer systems, privacy, seasonal peaks, vehicle and housing liability, low managerial bandwidth, and the need for human exception handling limit realized substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-season deployments at U.S. farm labor contractors reporting adoption cost, worker and customer usage, payroll error rates, dispatcher and recruiter hours, compliance findings, retention, and realized labor savings would narrow the estimate.
caveats: Compliance automation can reduce errors without reducing payroll or headcount.
Capabilities and economics differ sharply between small crew leaders, multistate H-2A labor contractors, transport and housing operators, and harvesting crews.

e.rationale: The frozen input supplies only 39 estimated LMM firms and no audited firm-level distribution. Active federal registration is a useful operating signal, but the DOL list includes both contractors and contractor employees, certain small businesses and agricultural employers are exempt, and legal registration does not establish primary NAICS, EBITDA, independence, transferability, or owner economics. Seasonal volatility, customer concentration, thin spread over wage pass-throughs, informal crew leaders, mixed harvesting services, captive associations, and working-capital needs reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A deduplicated match of active FLC certificates to Census businesses, legal entities, ownership, primary NAICS, five-year revenue and normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, exemptions, transport and housing authorizations, and transaction history would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The public registry is person-and-certificate based and cannot be equated to transferable firms.
The frozen 39-firm count is an estimate and should not be treated as a verified acquisition universe.

s5.bridge: An SBA article reports that 47% of owners expecting to retire within five years lacked a successor, but that statistic covers family businesses generally rather than labor contractors. I translate broad small-business succession pressure into five-year control-sale availability, then discount for young or mobile owner-operators, family or employee succession, certificate dependence on individuals, customer relationships that may not transfer, closures, and informal operations.
s5.rationale: Owner retirement, compliance burden, seasonal cash-flow strain, customer concentration, consolidation, and the value of an established worker network can create sales. Many crew-leader businesses instead close, transfer informally, lose workers or customers, or pass within families without a qualifying transaction.
s5.change_evidence: An owner-age, successor, sale-intent, entity-transferability, and exit-outcome survey of active FLC businesses, linked to size and certificate status, would replace the proxy.
caveats: The SBA statistic is an old cross-industry secondary proxy, not a current measured exit rate.
A contractor certificate, worker network, vehicles, housing rights, and customer contracts may transfer differently or require new approvals.

q.bridge: DOL rules make contractors responsible for accurate payroll records, worker disclosures, wage payment, registration, and activity-specific transport or housing authorization, while ERS describes persistent farm-labor scarcity and rapidly expanding H-2A use. I translate that operating structure into benefit retention: administrative automation can initially increase contractor spread or service quality, but growers compare crew costs closely, worker wages and mandated costs dominate, contracts may pass through savings, and competitors can adopt similar software.
q.rationale: Contractors may retain value through faster crew fulfillment, fewer payroll and compliance errors, better worker retention, reliable transport, and differentiated multilingual service. Competitive bidding, grower bargaining, wage and housing mandates, customer concentration, legal exposure, and technology diffusion shift much of the benefit to farms, workers, or compliance cost avoidance.
q.change_evidence: Representative customer contracts and bids showing fee formulas, wage and cost pass-throughs, renewal pricing, customer concentration, service-level penalties, and margin changes after workflow automation would materially change the range.
caveats: No public evidence directly measures five-year retention of automation savings by eligible contractors.
Per-hour, piece-rate, per-acre, H-2A, transport, housing, and full-crew contracts have different pass-through behavior.

d5.bridge: BLS projects total agricultural-worker employment down 3% from 2024 to 2034 as mechanization raises productivity, but still expects about 116,200 openings annually. USDA ERS reports H-2A positions increased from just over 48,000 in fiscal 2005 to about 385,000 in fiscal 2024, calling that growth an indicator of farm-labor scarcity. I translate these opposing signals into five-year demand for contractor-supplied labor: physical worker demand may soften, while scarcity, churn, regulatory complexity, and growers' outsourcing of recruiting and compliance can raise the contractor share.
d5.rationale: Labor scarcity, seasonal peaks, H-2A growth, worker churn, complex payroll, and growers' desire to outsource recruitment and transport support contractor demand. Mechanization, robotic harvesting, immigration or visa changes, grower consolidation, direct hiring, crop shifts, and farm closures create downside.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of outsourced farm-labor spending and contractor-supplied worker-hours, segmented by crop, region, H-2A status, mechanizability, wage model, and direct versus contractor employment, would materially improve the estimate.
caveats: H-2A certified positions are not identical to jobs filled or contractor revenue, and employers can use the program directly.
Occupational projections measure workers across industries, not demand for NAICS 115115 firms.

o.rationale: Even with digital recruiting and scheduling platforms, farms using migrant or seasonal contractors must verify a valid certificate, and contractors remain accountable for registrations, disclosures, payroll records, wages, authorizations, transport, housing, supervision, and worker supply. Some growers, associations, platforms, professional employer organizations, or direct H-2A programs can internalize or unbundle these functions, but software alone cannot employ, transport, house, supervise, or furnish a reliable field crew.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that farms or digital labor platforms can legally and operationally replace registered contractors at scale—including worker employment, payroll, transport, housing, supervision, and field fulfillment—would lower the estimate.
caveats: Direct hiring and agricultural-association exemptions can displace contractors without software becoming the supplier.
A platform may capture recruiting and matching value while a licensed operating entity continues to bear employment and compliance responsibility.

narrative.executive_view: Farm labor contractors have meaningful AI opportunity in recruiting, matching, scheduling, translation, payroll, routing, and compliance, but most wages fund physical field workers and the frozen 2.2759 labor-intensity ratio is not economically interpretable. Labor scarcity and H-2A growth support outsourcing even as mechanization limits total worker demand.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate worker intake, document review, multilingual communication, demand forecasting, crew assembly, time and piece-rate reconciliation, payroll, disclosures, authorization checks, vehicle routing, and compliance exception handling. Planting, pruning, harvesting, loading, driving, housing operation, emergency response, and crew supervision remain human and physical.
narrative.value_capture: Reliable fulfillment and fewer errors can protect contractor spread and customer retention, but wages and mandated costs dominate, growers bargain hard, savings may pass through, and competitors can adopt the same workflows.
narrative.firm_availability: Active registration signals a real operating business but not an eligible acquisition target. Certificate holders include employees, exemptions exist, the frozen count is estimated, and independence, EBITDA, transferability, customer concentration, and normalized owner economics remain unknown.
narrative.demand_durability: Mechanization pressures field-worker demand, while chronic scarcity, replacement openings, H-2A growth, seasonal peaks, and regulatory complexity can increase the share outsourced to contractors. A modest five-year increase is plausible but policy and crop exposure make the range wide.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Risks include immigration and H-2A policy, wage mandates, joint-employment liability, worker safety, housing and transport compliance, rural connectivity, payroll and piece-rate errors, customer concentration, seasonality, crop losses, mechanization, working capital, and the frozen wage-to-receipts measurement anomaly.
narrative.principal_driver: Scarce seasonal labor and expanding H-2A use increase the value of compliant recruiting, crew assembly, payroll, transport, and worker coordination.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most cost and work sit in physical field labor, while thin pass-through economics, legal exposure, and unreliable firm-level measurement constrain automation value and acquisition confidence.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: 115115 Farm Labor Contractors and Crew Leaders
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily supplying labor for agricultural production or harvesting and distinguishes machine harvesting and farm management.

sources.title: May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 115100
sources.supports: Reports 337,310 workers in broader crop-support activities, including 67.2% farming occupations, 56.1% crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers, and 2.67% management.

sources.title: MSPA Registered Farm Labor Contractor Listing
sources.supports: Explains federal certification, the active registry, certificate holders, expirations, and housing, transport, and driving authorizations.

sources.title: MSPA Certificate Registration Frequently Asked Questions
sources.supports: Documents registration requirements for farm labor contractors and employees and the active-certificate listing.

sources.title: Fact Sheet #49: Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act
sources.supports: Documents contractor registration, payroll records, wage and disclosure duties, proof of registration, and specific transport and housing authorizations.

sources.title: Fact Sheet #26: H-2A Temporary Agricultural Workers
sources.supports: Documents employer and H-2A labor-contractor obligations, including recruitment, wages, housing, transport, records, and FLC certificates and authorizations.

sources.title: U.S. H-2A Positions Certified by State, Fiscal Years 2005-24
sources.supports: Reports certified positions increasing more than sevenfold from just over 48,000 in fiscal 2005 to about 385,000 in fiscal 2024 as an indicator of farm-labor scarcity.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Agricultural Workers
sources.supports: Projects employment down 3% from 2024 to 2034 but about 116,200 annual replacement openings, and describes mechanization as limiting labor demand.

sources.title: 5 Challenges for Family-Owned Businesses
sources.supports: Provides a broad succession proxy: 47% of owners expecting retirement within five years reportedly lacked a successor.

sources.title: Food and Agricultural Industries: Opportunities for Improving Measurement and Reporting
sources.supports: Explains frame and worker-coverage complications for farm labor contractors, including treatment of agricultural production workers and sole proprietors in Census and BLS data.
